chapters.title: Even cynics need something to chew on
chapters.text:
Many philosophical works have lasted through the centuries, but one stands out for the clarity of its voice. Compared with the dense style of most philosophy, Nietzsche is concise, lively, and a pleasure to read. He does not push a single ideology. Instead, he asks his readers to question the prevailing assumptions of the modern world.

Nietzsche drew from a range of influences, including Arthur Schopenhauer, Richard Wagner, and Paul Ree. Schopenhauer, a respected philosopher and committed atheist, gave Nietzsche early writings a deep religious tone. Wagner, the famous composer, and Ree, a fellow philosopher, shaped his thinking on suffering and morality. The friendships mattered to his philosophy, and when he broke with Wagner, he was forced to rethink many of his ideas.

Morality is not fixed. It shifts with politics, culture, and the level of enlightenment in a society.

Around that time, Nietzsche retreated into a more solitary life, which changed the direction of his work. He stopped avoiding the hard questions about the human will to live, to exist, to create, and to push into the unknown. He argued for accepting life, with all of its suffering, as the only way to live fully.

For most of his life, Nietzsche pushed back against nearly every doctrine and ethic that shaped modern Europe. By the end of his life, his long series of rejections had led him to say yes, yes to life, to its hardships, to the gaps between social classes, to pain, and to the certainty of a hard life.

Nietzsche earned wide recognition as a great thinker, and his writings now present the full scope of his mind. Read on to step into his visions of morality in a civilized society.

chapters.title: Philosophical prejudice and the free spirited thinker
chapters.text:
Philosophers love to shape life in their own image and to spread their views to anyone who will listen. Nietzsche, for one, treated Stoicism as a form of self imposed tyranny, one that ignores the value of personal passion and pain. In his view, it was a kind of self humiliation, performed to project superiority and power over others.

Philosophers have built castles in the air while pretending to develop sound theories. When they came back to earth, they often arrived with questions that could have been answered in a sentence. Nietzsche saw his peers as defenders of their own position, which makes sense given how he read his intellectual ancestors and contemporaries.

He also believed that some philosophers had quietly justified criminals in their work. The reasoning is that philosophy asks for a balanced view of all human effort, moral and immoral. Thinkers pick a side to claim their niche, and a few go so far as to explore corrupted minds and offer excuses for them.

A popular belief mixes up the search for knowledge with philosophy, but they are not the same.

As a member of that class, Nietzsche was not shy about his view of humanity. He made it clear that he did not approve of the way society handled itself. He thought people avoided the hard parts of life, especially suffering, and in doing so they kept themselves from becoming complex. He pointed out that the average person will accept dogmas about God and how God deals with humans, with all the usual stereotypes, and yet that same person will twist scripture whenever it does not match their preferences.

Nietzsche championed human autonomy, and he insisted that the walls of morality should not get in the way of a person discovering who they are and what they can be. He also argued that the chase for truth is wasted on thinkers, naive observers, moralists, and the public. For him, truth is personal and shaped by individual belief. Even with all that, his ideas received only limited attention during his lifetime. Had he known how widely he would be read after death, he might have expressed himself with a little more restraint.

chapters.title: The thinker who explained Heaven
chapters.text:
The eighteenth century brought the industrial revolution and a reshaping of the economy, which changed how people lived and what they believed, including religion. Religion could not avoid the moment, and that shift shows up in the literature and philosophy of the period. When it came to faith, Nietzsche was the one to call out its basic character.

He pushed hard against what he saw as a loss of spirituality, especially among European Christians of his time. As a freethinker, he believed that many used religion as a convenient channel to push their own will while ignoring its rules and structure. He also raised a paradox: even though religion promotes virtues like spirituality, humility, and modesty, it can also blunt a person innate potential and lower their capabilities and aspirations.

Nietzsche thought that many religious leaders were short on intellect and originality, but he did not dismiss the Church entirely, since it had contributed to Europe development. He did argue, though, that humanity would do a better job of judgment and morality if people were allowed to follow their own intuition.

Faith gives us joy, hope, and optimism in a cruel world.

Nietzsche drew from a wide range of texts, including the Bible, and many of his lines echo Scripture. One statement, for instance, captures how he saw the link between religion and intellect. In his view, every person, no matter what they believe about God, is trying to assert their influence, power, principles, and personal tastes in everything they do.

He never argued for the abolition of religion. He attacked shallowness, pleasure, and patriotism as distractions from higher values. To him, Christianity encouraged weakness, fragility, and the acceptance of being ruled. He believed Europe would eventually decay if Christian values kept their grip, which is the heart of his claim that "God is dead" in the eyes of his followers.

Morality is so deeply rooted that it will outlive the death of religion, as Friedrich Nietzsche has written.

A quick fact: Nietzsche was in poor health for most of his life, and his mind deteriorated sharply after he watched an old horse being whipped in the street. His thinking on morality went beyond humanity and reached into life itself.

chapters.title: On the history of morals and the scholar trouble
chapters.text:
Nietzsche did not build moral systems in his own philosophy. He actually rejected morality as something with no value, arguing that it kept capable people from pursuing the best form of life. In his view, morals helped people survive, but they did not help them live.

Societies naturally arrange themselves around rules that work well for keeping things stable. That balance can collapse when arbitrary regulations are forced on people in the name of connection. Modern Europe shows what happens when the scales of morality are forced, suggesting that some legalistic thinkers see humans as a slightly higher form of animal that will run loose without constant supervision.

Nietzsche found the act of building societal codes disrespectful. He believed that, no matter the structures in play, hierarchies would form naturally based on talent, ability, and wealth. For example, the push for equality, treated as a pillar of morality, is doomed to fail because it goes against the laws of nature. We wish for equal treatment for everyone, but does that ever actually happen? Those are the kinds of gaps Nietzsche found in institutionalized morality.

Philosophers are often misunderstood, looking mysterious and cryptic, and only a few have made a mark as self assertive. Instead of direct proclamations, they quietly steer others to see the world their way. Nietzsche often said he would rather be misunderstood, or rather be understood only with effort. He thought the fire of philosophy might die out in the future. As a result, the philosophers of his time, he felt, avoided skepticism, preferring to keep the dogmas and ethics of the day intact.

Philosophy is hard, even impossible, to teach, since it is a path of self discovery that takes each person to a different place.

Nietzsche also thought most people lumped philosophers and scientists together. He wanted to make clear that philosophers cover many undefined specialties, and that they are knowledge in human form. Scientists, on the other hand, do not have the same freedom. They tend to specialize and work under the dogmas of their field.

chapters.title: Virtues change with the caste
chapters.text:
Virtues have always set the cultural and moral direction of a civilization. The values we live with today come from the influences around us. The moral instincts of our ancestors have rubbed off on us. Even when we respect their integrity, we keep an eye out for their lapses. So Nietzsche advises a virtuous soul not to make the less moral feel guilty for falling short.

Virtue can be collective or individual. In a group, the collective worth tends to win out over individual preference.

From his philosophical perch, Nietzsche argues that being righteous can actually backfire. He believes that some roles demand the assertiveness of a brute to push people toward a goal. A military dictator, for example, cannot afford to be pro virtue, since that goes against the point of the role.

He extended the same idea to gender roles and equality. He wrote about how women are driven to seek careers and build a respectable image for themselves. Although he wrote in the nineteenth century, his words read like a prediction of twenty first century women gaining more rights and opportunities, thanks to earlier advocates and their own efforts. That change is largely the result of democracy spreading around the world. Do not mistake this for a compliment, though. Nietzsche was a clear misogynist. To him, women efforts to emancipate themselves were laughable, and their refusal to be charming was almost offensive.

He also examined how nineteenth century European democracies treated Jewish people, which revealed the existence of collective virtue, a shared ethical standard within a group. Nietzsche acknowledged that the presence of Jewish people in European countries had its advantages, especially for intellectuals like him, since they brought a wider cultural range. He also admitted that the average German of his time was hostile to Jewish people, and that German politicians could have eased some of the tension by managing the influx the way some other European countries did.

Love sits above any category of morality. It is neither good nor evil. It just is, and that is its strength, as Friedrich Nietzsche has noted.

chapters.title: The strange label of nobility
chapters.text:
The rise and fall of power has always set off historical shifts, and the shifts have often meant the repression of one group and the rise of another. That pattern drains the less privileged, since groups like oligarchies hold long term influence. Even with progress and the end of slavery, the order of inversions still stands in subtler forms. It is telling that Nietzsche and thinkers like him saw hints of this as the future of humanity.

He did not believe the brutal injustices of the past, or the present, came from a lack of civilization. Instead, he argued that oligarchies are the fabric of society, and they have to be watched and prevented from swinging to either extreme, lawlessness on one side and anarchy on the other.

In his search for a general rule of morality that would apply to human society, Nietzsche could not find a single virtue that was fixed. He sorted most virtues into two kinds: master oriented and servant oriented. Under any condition, in any era or culture, he thought morality came down to one of these two types.

Master oriented morality includes strength, ambition, daring, and open mindedness. It goes beyond the simple labels of good and evil. We should cherish the first and root out the second. For Nietzsche, noble and powerful people live by the master oriented morality, while the weak, the cowardly, and the narrow minded are bound to the servant oriented kind. The servants do not try to meet their masters through growth. They try to vilify the virtues the masters have built. As a result, the servants Nietzsche describes are cynical and pessimistic.

Being noble often means accepting a healthy dose of egoism, since nobility rarely comes with servility.

A noble soul, however, has likely accepted the need to exercise its will to power, a concept Nietzsche developed, even when it appears humble or pious. The noble person knows that troubled people will be left in their wake. Every person a noble soul meets is seen as a helper, a hurdle, or a refuge.

He also criticized philosophers who claimed to be masters in the pure pursuit of truth. He believed the best philosophers wove their values, their morality, and their nuances into their teachings. Nietzsche was no exception. After all, you practice what you preach.

chapters.title: Closing thoughts
chapters.text:
When it comes to the self, Nietzsche took philosophy to task for missing the mark on consciousness and ethics. He pictured humans as caught between choice and destiny, and he put the notion of free will under the microscope. On the question of value, he saw that moral codes drift with culture, geography, the spread of ideas, and the era. Watching those rules shift constantly, he asked why we cling to them so tightly.

When it comes to suffering and compassion, he laid bare how fragile goodness, values, and morality really are. Tenderness can be handed to the vulnerable, while the strong use it to draw them into line. To him, suffering was a gift that pointed toward a fuller and more grown up purpose.

When it comes to faith, Nietzsche is behind the well known line "God is dead," which many twenty first century atheists have claimed as their own. Most of them use the quote without its context. The full line reads "God is dead, and we have killed him," pointing to a broken view of the divine.

A pupa wriggling to escape its cocoon and become a butterfly is a useful image. In the garden where the change was unfolding, the gardener saw the struggle and pulled the insect free. The butterfly died soon after. A biologist friend later explained that the help during that symbolic stage likely stripped the butterfly of its built in drive to live. Nietzsche worked the same insight into his notion of the will to power. He argued that people gain intellectual, religious, physical, and even political strength only after they have been tempered by the fire of suffering.
